title: JNodeProperties

tex_statement.html: With the notations def_3_2 the restrictions in definition def_3_1 mean that the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any arrowheads pointing towards them: $ j \hus v \notin G$. Nodes $j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$: edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed. Furthermore, no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.

tex_proof.raw:
% Claim proof file for row claim_3_1 -- "JNodeProperties".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. A
% manager agent dedicated to writing the TeX proof fills in the body below.
% The proof must:
%   - Stay close to the lecture notes' proof if one exists (always search
%     the LN first for a `\begin{proof}` block immediately following the
%     claim; copy / lightly edit when present).
%   - Otherwise use the LN paradigm and cite prior claims by their `ref`.
%   - Be self-contained enough that a mathematician can follow it without
%     re-reading the LN.
%   - Have no `sorry`, `omitted`, or "obvious" shortcuts.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_1 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: JNodeProperties
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_1}
\def\rowtitle{JNodeProperties}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_1}

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Rem}
With the notations \refrow{def_3_2} the restrictions in definition \refrow{def_3_1} mean that the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any arrowheads pointing towards them: $ j \hus v \notin G$. Nodes $j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$: edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed. Furthermore, no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.
\end{Rem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
% The LN states this as a remark immediately after \refrow{def_3_2}
% (graphs.tex line 84-89) and provides no proof block; the statement is
% the unfolding-only consequence of the structural fields of
% \refrow{def_3_1}. The proof below is constructed from scratch in the
% LN paradigm: every step is one notation-unfolding of \refrow{def_3_2}
% followed by one of the inclusions $E \ins (J \cup V) \x V$,
% $L \ins V \x V$, or the disjointness $J \cap V = \emptyset$ from
% \refrow{def_3_1}.

\begin{proof}
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG as in \refrow{def_3_1}. We verify the
three assertions in turn.

\medskip
\noindent\emph{(1) No arrowhead points at any $j \in J$:
$j \hus v \notin G$ for all $v \in G$.}
Suppose for contradiction that $j \in J$ and $j \hus v \in G$ for some
$v$. By \refrow{def_3_2}, $j \hus v \in G$ means that either
$j \hut v \in G$ or $j \huh v \in G$.
\begin{itemize}
  \item If $j \hut v \in G$, then by \refrow{def_3_2} $(v, j) \in E$.
        By \refrow{def_3_1} we have $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$,
        so the second coordinate of any pair in $E$ lies in $V$; hence
        $j \in V$.
  \item If $j \huh v \in G$, then by \refrow{def_3_2} $(j, v) \in L$.
        By \refrow{def_3_1} we have $L \ins V \times V$, so both
        coordinates of any pair in $L$ lie in $V$; hence $j \in V$.
\end{itemize}
Either way $j \in J \cap V$, contradicting the disjointness of $J$ and
$V$ stipulated in \refrow{def_3_1}.

\medskip
\noindent\emph{(2) Directed edges out of an input node target $V$:
if $j \in J$ and $j \tuh w \in G$, then $w \in V$.}
By \refrow{def_3_2}, $j \tuh w \in G$ means $(j, w) \in E$. Since
$E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ by \refrow{def_3_1}, the second coordinate
$w$ lies in $V$.

\medskip
\noindent\emph{(3) No two input nodes are adjacent: for all
$j_1, j_2 \in J$, $j_1 \sus j_2 \notin G$.}
Suppose for contradiction that $j_1, j_2 \in J$ and
$j_1 \sus j_2 \in G$. By \refrow{def_3_2}, $j_1 \sus j_2 \in G$ means
one of the three sub-cases $j_1 \tuh j_2 \in G$,
$j_1 \hut j_2 \in G$, or $j_1 \huh j_2 \in G$.
\begin{itemize}
  \item If $j_1 \tuh j_2 \in G$, then $(j_1, j_2) \in E$, which by
        \refrow{def_3_2} is exactly $j_2 \hut j_1 \in G$. In particular
        $j_2 \hus j_1 \in G$, contradicting part (1) applied to
        $j_2 \in J$.
  \item If $j_1 \hut j_2 \in G$, then $j_1 \hus j_2 \in G$ directly
        by the definition of $\hus$ in \refrow{def_3_2}, contradicting
        part (1) applied to $j_1 \in J$.
  \item If $j_1 \huh j_2 \in G$, then $j_1 \hus j_2 \in G$ (again
        directly from \refrow{def_3_2}), contradicting part (1)
        applied to $j_1 \in J$.
\end{itemize}
In each case we reach a contradiction, so $j_1 \sus j_2 \notin G$; by
the adjacency definition immediately following \refrow{def_3_2}, this
is precisely the statement that no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Remark

With the notations def_3_2 the restrictions in definition def_3_1 mean that the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any arrowheads pointing towards them: $ j \hus v \notin G$. Nodes $j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$: edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed. Furthermore, no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.

Proof.

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG as in def_3_1. We verify the three assertions in turn.

\medskip \noindent(1) No arrowhead points at any $j \in J$: $j \hus v \notin G$ for all $v \in G$. Suppose for contradiction that $j \in J$ and $j \hus v \in G$ for some $v$. By def_3_2, $j \hus v \in G$ means that either $j \hut v \in G$ or $j \huh v \in G$.

  • If $j \hut v \in G$, then by def_3_2 $(v, j) \in E$. By def_3_1 we have $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$, so the second coordinate of any pair in $E$ lies in $V$; hence $j \in V$.
  • If $j \huh v \in G$, then by def_3_2 $(j, v) \in L$. By def_3_1 we have $L \ins V \times V$, so both coordinates of any pair in $L$ lie in $V$; hence $j \in V$.

Either way $j \in J \cap V$, contradicting the disjointness of $J$ and $V$ stipulated in def_3_1.

\medskip \noindent(2) Directed edges out of an input node target $V$: if $j \in J$ and $j \tuh w \in G$, then $w \in V$. By def_3_2, $j \tuh w \in G$ means $(j, w) \in E$. Since $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ by def_3_1, the second coordinate $w$ lies in $V$.

\medskip \noindent(3) No two input nodes are adjacent: for all $j_1, j_2 \in J$, $j_1 \sus j_2 \notin G$. Suppose for contradiction that $j_1, j_2 \in J$ and $j_1 \sus j_2 \in G$. By def_3_2, $j_1 \sus j_2 \in G$ means one of the three sub-cases $j_1 \tuh j_2 \in G$, $j_1 \hut j_2 \in G$, or $j_1 \huh j_2 \in G$.

  • If $j_1 \tuh j_2 \in G$, then $(j_1, j_2) \in E$, which by def_3_2 is exactly $j_2 \hut j_1 \in G$. In particular $j_2 \hus j_1 \in G$, contradicting part (1) applied to $j_2 \in J$.
  • If $j_1 \hut j_2 \in G$, then $j_1 \hus j_2 \in G$ directly by the definition of $\hus$ in def_3_2, contradicting part (1) applied to $j_1 \in J$.
  • If $j_1 \huh j_2 \in G$, then $j_1 \hus j_2 \in G$ (again directly from def_3_2), contradicting part (1) applied to $j_1 \in J$.

In each case we reach a contradiction, so $j_1 \sus j_2 \notin G$; by the adjacency definition immediately following def_3_2, this is precisely the statement that no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.

lean.title: JNodeProperties -- no arrowheads into an input node
lean.comments:
--
-- For any input node `j ∈ G.J` and any vertex `v : α`, there is no
-- edge with an arrowhead at `j`. The LN writes this as
-- `j \hus v \notin G`, which in our notation is `¬ hus G j v` --
-- recalling that `hus G j v` is "arrowhead at the first argument",
-- i.e. either `hut G j v` (a directed edge `j ← v`) or `huh G j v`
-- (a bidirected edge `j ↔ v`). The fact follows from `E_subset`
-- (every directed-edge target lives in `V`) and `L_subset`
-- (bidirected edges live in `V × V`), combined with `disjoint_JV`
-- to derive `j ∉ V` from `j ∈ J`.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex`:

\begin{Rem}
With the notations \ref{not-cdmg} the restrictions in definition
\ref{def-cdmg} mean that the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any
arrowheads pointing towards them: $ j \hus v \notin G$. Nodes
$j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$: edges $j \tuh v$
are allowed. Furthermore, no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.
\end{Rem}
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **Three separate theorems, not one conjunction.** The LN
--   bundles three independent sub-statements into one prose
--   paragraph for readability, but in Lean each one is wanted as a
--   *separately citable* lemma. A downstream proof using "directed
--   edges out of J target V" (part 2) almost never needs "no
--   arrowhead at J" (part 1) on the same line. Splitting them
--   into three theorems keeps each one a clean one-liner at the
--   use site and avoids the boilerplate of
--   `obtain ⟨h₁, h₂, h₃⟩ := …`.
--
-- * **`v` explicit.** The LN reads "for any $v$, $j \hus v \notin
--   G$": the universal quantifier is outside `hus`. Making `v`
--   explicit keeps the call-site phrasing `no_arrowhead_into_input
--   hj v` aligned with that reading. Callers that have only
--   `h : hus G j v` and want a contradiction will write
--   `no_arrowhead_into_input hj v h` directly. The alternative
--   (implicit `v`) would force the caller to either provide it
--   via `@`-syntax or let Lean unify against `h`'s type, which is
--   less readable.
--
-- * **`G` implicit, inferred from `hj : j ∈ G.J`.** Standard for
--   "fix a graph, then state a property of it" lemmas.
--
-- * **Phrased in terms of `hus`, not raw `G.E` / `G.L`
--   membership.** The LN's notation `j \hus v` is the prose-level
--   atom of the claim; downstream proofs that want to derive a
--   contradiction from an edge of either kind will already be
--   working with `hus`, `suh`, or `Adjacent`. Spelling the
--   conclusion in terms of `hus` lets them apply this lemma
--   without an extra unfolding step.
lean.statement:
theorem no_arrowhead_into_input
    {G : CDMG α} {j : α} (hj : j ∈ G.J) (v : α) :
    ¬ hus G j v := by
lean.proof:
intro hhus
  have hjV : j ∈ G.V := by
    rcases hhus with h_hut | h_huh
    · exact (G.E_subset h_hut).2
    · exact (G.L_subset h_huh).1
  exact Set.disjoint_left.mp G.disjoint_JV hj hjV

lean.title: JNodeProperties -- directed edges out of J target V
lean.comments:
--
-- For any input node `j ∈ G.J` and any vertex `w` such that there
-- is a directed edge `j \tuh w` in `G`, the target `w` lies in
-- `G.V`. The LN phrases this as "edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed
-- [only when $v \in V$]"; we state it as the implication.
-- Follows from `E_subset : G.E ⊆ (J ∪ V) ×ˢ V`.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex`:

\begin{Rem}
With the notations \ref{not-cdmg} the restrictions in definition
\ref{def-cdmg} mean that the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any
arrowheads pointing towards them: $ j \hus v \notin G$. Nodes
$j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$: edges $j \tuh v$
are allowed. Furthermore, no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.
\end{Rem}
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **Phrased as `tuh G j w → w ∈ G.V`, not as raw
--   `(j, w) ∈ G.E → w ∈ G.V`.** The LN's notation `j \tuh v` is
--   the prose-level atom (def_3_2); stating the conclusion in
--   terms of `tuh` lines up with what callers actually have in
--   hand. Unfolding to `(j, w) ∈ G.E` is one rewrite away via the
--   `tuh` definition, but that's the proof's business, not the
--   statement's.
--
-- * **No need for a separate "no `hut G j w` for `w ∈ J`" lemma.**
--   `hut G j w` is `(w, j) ∈ G.E`, which by `E_subset` forces
--   `j ∈ V`, contradicting `j ∈ J` via `disjoint_JV`. That's
--   exactly part (1) above specialised to the `hut`-disjunct of
--   `hus`, so it is already a consequence of
--   `no_arrowhead_into_input` and does not warrant its own
--   theorem.
--
-- * **`hj` explicit, `w` implicit.** `hj : j ∈ G.J` is the
--   side-condition the caller is providing; `w` is determined by
--   the edge hypothesis `h : tuh G j w` and so can be left to
--   unification. Note that the *proof* below does not use `hj` --
--   the inclusion `E ⊆ (J ∪ V) ×ˢ V` is strong enough on its own
--   to force `w ∈ V`. We still take `hj` as an explicit argument
--   because the lemma is prose-named for input nodes: removing
--   `hj` would broaden the statement to "the target of any
--   directed edge lies in `V`", which is a different lemma. The
--   `set_option` below silences the unused-variable linter for
--   exactly this case.
set_option linter.unusedVariables false in

lean.title: JNodeProperties -- no two input nodes are adjacent
lean.comments:
--
-- For any two input nodes `j₁, j₂ ∈ G.J`, there is no edge of any
-- kind between them. By def_3_3, `Adjacent G j₁ j₂` is the prose
-- name for `sus G j₁ j₂`, which is the disjunction of
-- `tuh G j₁ j₂`, `hut G j₁ j₂`, and `huh G j₁ j₂`. Each disjunct
-- is forbidden by the structural restrictions of def_3_1:
--   * `tuh G j₁ j₂` forces `j₂ ∈ V` (via `E_subset`),
--     contradicting `j₂ ∈ J` and `disjoint_JV`.
--   * `hut G j₁ j₂` symmetrically forces `j₁ ∈ V`.
--   * `huh G j₁ j₂` forces both `j₁ ∈ V` and `j₂ ∈ V` (via
--     `L_subset`).
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex`:

\begin{Rem}
With the notations \ref{not-cdmg} the restrictions in definition
\ref{def-cdmg} mean that the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any
arrowheads pointing towards them: $ j \hus v \notin G$. Nodes
$j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$: edges $j \tuh v$
are allowed. Furthermore, no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.
\end{Rem}
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **Phrased with `Adjacent`, not `sus`.** def_3_3 introduces
--   `Adjacent` as the prose-level name for the LN's "adjacent in
--   $G$"; the LN sentence "no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent"
--   literally uses that word. Stating the conclusion in terms of
--   `Adjacent` matches the LN phrasing and lets downstream callers
--   keep their proofs at the prose layer rather than unfolding to
--   `sus`. `Adjacent` is definitionally `sus` (see
--   `EdgeRelations.adjacent_iff`), so this choice does not change
--   the proof obligation.
--
-- * **Both `j₁` and `j₂` implicit.** Both are pinned down by the
--   adjacency hypothesis the caller would feed into the negation
--   (or by the goal they are trying to close), so making either
--   explicit would just be call-site noise.
--
-- * **No `j₁ ≠ j₂` hypothesis.** The LN's "no two nodes" reads as
--   "for any two (possibly equal) input nodes, they are not
--   adjacent". When `j₁ = j₂`, `Adjacent G j₁ j₁` is still false:
--   the `tuh` / `hut` disjuncts both reduce to `(j₁, j₁) ∈ G.E`,
--   which by `E_subset` would force `j₁ ∈ V` (contradicting
--   `j₁ ∈ J` via `disjoint_JV`); the `huh` disjunct is ruled out
--   directly by `L_irrefl`. So adding `j₁ ≠ j₂` would weaken the
--   lemma unnecessarily.
lean.statement:
theorem input_nodes_not_adjacent
    {G : CDMG α} {j₁ j₂ : α}
    (h₁ : j₁ ∈ G.J) (h₂ : j₂ ∈ G.J) :
    ¬ Adjacent G j₁ j₂ := by
lean.proof:
intro hAdj
  rcases hAdj with h_tuh | h_hut | h_huh
  · exact no_arrowhead_into_input h₂ j₁ (Or.inl h_tuh)
  · exact no_arrowhead_into_input h₁ j₂ (Or.inl h_hut)
  · exact no_arrowhead_into_input h₁ j₂ (Or.inr h_huh)

lean_explanation:
The remark bundles three independent sub-statements, so the Lean formalisation diverges structurally by splitting them into three separately-citable theorems: `no_arrowhead_into_input` (no arrowhead lands at $j \in J$), `input_edge_target_mem_V` (directed edges from $J$ target $V$), and `input_nodes_not_adjacent` (no two input nodes are adjacent). The identifier names mirror the prose: "arrowhead into input" tracks the LN's `j \hus v`, "edge target mem V" is the projection conclusion, and "not adjacent" matches the LN word `Adjacent`.

All three share the same engine: the structural fields `G.E_subset : G.E ⊆ (J ∪ V) ×ˢ V`, `G.L_subset : G.L ⊆ V ×ˢ V`, and `G.disjoint_JV`. Mathlib pieces worth noting:

- `×ˢ` is `Set.prod`, the set-level Cartesian product appearing in the subset hypotheses.
- `Set.disjoint_left.mp` turns `Disjoint G.J G.V` into the "no element lies in both" form, closing part 1 via `G.disjoint_JV hj hjV`.
- `rcases h with a | b` destructures `Or`; part 1 splits `hus` into its `hut`/`huh` disjuncts, part 3 splits `Adjacent` (definitionally `sus`) into three disjuncts.
- `.1` / `.2` project the coordinates from membership in a `×ˢ` set.

Part 2 is a one-line term-mode proof `(G.E_subset h).2`. Part 3 reuses part 1 by re-wrapping each adjacency disjunct via `Or.inl` / `Or.inr` into the `hus` shape; the `tuh` case is dispatched at `j₂` (the arrowhead's target) while the `hut` and `huh` cases are dispatched at `j₁`.
design_choices:
The Lean comments justify splitting the remark into three theorems rather than one conjunction on citability grounds: downstream proofs almost never need more than one sub-statement at a time, and separate lemmas avoid forcing callers to write `obtain ⟨h₁, h₂, h₃⟩ := …` boilerplate at every use site.

Argument-binding choices are tuned to call-site phrasing. In part 1, `v` is kept explicit so a caller with `h : hus G j v` can write `no_arrowhead_into_input hj v h` directly, matching the LN's "for any $v$" reading; making `v` implicit would force `@`-syntax or fragile unification. In part 2, `hj : j ∈ G.J` is kept explicit even though the proof does not consume it — dropping it would broaden the statement into a different, weaker lemma about arbitrary directed edges — and the unused-variable linter is silenced via `set_option linter.unusedVariables false in`. In part 3, both `j₁` and `j₂` are implicit because they are pinned down by the adjacency hypothesis. Across all three, `G` is implicit and inferred from the `j ∈ G.J` hypothesis, the standard "fix a graph, then state a property" convention.

Conclusions are phrased at the prose layer — `hus`, `tuh`, and `Adjacent` (definitionally `sus` via `EdgeRelations.adjacent_iff`) — so callers can apply the lemmas without an extra unfolding step. Part 3 deliberately omits a `j₁ ≠ j₂` hypothesis: the self-adjacency case is still false (the `huh` disjunct is ruled out by `L_irrefl`, the others by `E_subset` and `disjoint_JV`), so adding it would needlessly weaken the lemma. A separate "no `hut G j w` for `w ∈ J`" lemma is omitted as a redundant specialisation of part 1.
